content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

August 13, 1976


Mr. Art Campos
Bee Staff Writer
The Sacramento Bee
21st and Q Streets
Post Office Box 15779
Sacramento, California   95813

Dear Mr. Campos:

     Your letter of August 6 has been referred to me for reply.
You ask, hypothetically, whether a music teacher who owns a
business enterprise which repairs musical instruments would be
in conflict of interest if he or she recommended to his or her
employing district to contract with his or her company to re-
pair musical instruments owned by the district.  The answer is
yes.

     Government Code §87100 provides:

          No public official at any level of state
          or local government shall make, partici-
          pate in making or in any way attempt to
          use his official position to influence a
          governmental decision in which he knows
          or has reason to know he has a financial
          interest.

                              Emphasis added.

     Government Code §87103 provides:

          An official has a financial interest in
          a decision within the meaning of Section
          87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable
          that the decision will have a material
          financial effect, distinguishable from
          its effect on the public generally, on:

               (a) Any business entity in which the
               public official has a direct or indirect
               investment worth more than one thousand
               dollars ($1,000); ...


TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE/ADMINISTRATION (OIA) 777-5440 · EXECUTIVE (SCA) 322-5891 · [illegible]

Mr. Art Campos
August 13, 1976
Page Two


     (c) Any source of income, other than loans
by a commercial lending institution in the
regular course of business, aggregating two
hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
received by or promised to the public offi-
cial within twelve months prior to the time
when the decision is made; ...


     The Fair Political Practices Commission by regulation has
construed the concept "in any way attempt to use his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision" as
follows:

     ... furthering or attempting to affect in any
manner any decision:

     (1) Within or before his or her agency; or

     (2) Before any agency which is appointed by
or subject to the budgetary control of his or
her agency; ...

                              2 Cal. Adm. Code §18700(e)


     Thus, it appears clear that by using his or her official
position to influence the school district to employ his or her
company for the repair of musical instruments, the music teacher
is in violation of Government Code §87100.  This Section of the
Political Reform Act is operative against all public officials
in the State of California without regard to the existence of
a Conflict of Interest Code covering the agency of the official.

     The remedies for the violation of §87100 are directed to
injunctive action against the agency.  Government Code §91003(b)
provides:

     Upon a preliminary showing in an action
     brought by a person residing in the jur-
     isdiction that a violation of Article 1
     of Chapter 7 of this title or of a dis-
     qualification provision of a Conflict of
     Interest Code has occurred, the court may
     restrain the execution of any official
     action in relation to which such a vio-
     lation occurred, pending final adjudica-
     tion.  If it is ultimately determined

Mr. Art Campos
August 13, 1976
Page Three


that a violation has occurred and that the
official action might not otherwise have
been taken or approved, the court may set
the official action aside as void. The
official actions covered by this subsection
include but are not limited to orders, permits, resolutions and contracts, but do not
include the enactment of any state legislation. In considering the granting of preliminary or permanent relief under this
subsection, the court shall accord due
weight to any injury that may be suffered
by innocent persons relying on the official
action.


I hope this response has been of assistance to you. If
you have any further questions, please contact me at 916/322-
6444.

Sincerely,


Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr.
Chief, Conflicts of Interest
Division

DLS:gb

THE SACRAMENTO BEE

SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA

August 6, 1976

Jeanne Pritchard
Administrative Assistant
Fair Political Practice Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California 95819

Dear Ms. Pritchard:

As I mentioned to you over the telephone, I am interested in obtaining an opinion from your office over the possibility of a conflict of interest existing in a local school district.

The situation concerns a music teacher in a district who has formed his own company to repair musical instruments. The teacher has obtained a business license for that purpose. Some months after forming the company, the teacher requested his school district to employ his company in the repair of musical instruments.

The principal at his school approved the requisition slip and so did the district's business manager. Later, the school board okayed the expenditure. In subsequent months, the district employed the teacher's company three more times. However, the requisition forms in all three cases were made by music teachers from other schools in the district. Overall, the music teacher, who is a regularly salaried employee in that school district, was given over $1,300 by the district for services performed by his company.

I have questioned school officials and the music teacher about these expenditures and they have told me that no laws are being violated and that there is no conflict of interest. They base this on the fact that the district has no conflict of interest policy. I was under the impression that public agencies are required to adopt such a policy (as of April 1, 1976).

My questions to you are the following: Is the music teacher involved in a conflict of interest? Since the teacher can request the district to use his company's services, can he be considered to have some influence in the decision (see Govt. Code, Section 87100)? Is the district involved in a conflict of interest for knowingly awarding the contracts? Is it permitted to operate without a conflict of interest policy?

21st AND Q • P.O. BOX 15779 • SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95813 • TELEPHONE: 442-5011

THE SACRAMENTO BEE

SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA

I am certain you will find other points applicable to
this case which I have failed to ask. Should you find them,
please inform me. Your prompt attention to my questions
would be highly appreciated.

Sincerely,

Art Campos
Art Campos
Bee Staff Writer




21st AND Q • P.O. BOX 15779 • SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95813 • TELEPHONE: 442-5011

sections.question: Is the music teacher involved in a conflict of interest? Since the teacher can request the district to use his company's services, can he be considered to have some influence in the decision (see Govt. Code, Section 87100)? Is the district involved in a conflict of interest for knowingly awarding the contracts? Is it permitted to operate without a conflict of interest policy?
sections.conclusion: Thus, it appears clear that by using his or her official position to influence the school district to employ his or her company for the repair of musical instruments, the music teacher is in violation of Government Code §87100. This Section of the Political Reform Act is operative against all public officials in the State of California without regard to the existence of a Conflict of Interest Code covering the agency of the official.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a music teacher who owns a business repairing musical instruments violates conflict of interest laws by using his official position to influence his school district to contract with his company for instrument repairs.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The music teacher violates Government Code §87100 by using his official position to influence the district to employ his company for repairs. The Political Reform Act applies to all public officials regardless of whether the agency has adopted a conflict of interest code, and remedies include injunctive action and voiding of contracts.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request letter (from Art Campos, dated August 6, 1976) and the FPPC response (from Delbert L. Spurlock Jr., dated August 13, 1976). Some text is degraded/illegible in the source document but key sections are recoverable. The question and conclusion sections are clearly identifiable from the FPPC response portion.

QUESTION: Is the music teacher involved in a conflict of interest? Since the teacher can request the district to use his company's services, can he be considered to have some influence in the decision (see Govt. Code, Section 87100)? Is the district involved in a conflict of interest for knowingly awarding the contracts? Is it permitted to operate without a conflict of interest policy?

CONCLUSION: Thus, it appears clear that by using his or her official position to influence the school district to employ his or her company for the repair of musical instruments, the music teacher is in violation of Government Code §87100. This Section of the Political Reform Act is operative against all public officials in the State of California without regard to the existence of a Conflict of Interest Code covering the agency of the official.

embedding.summary: The FPPC responds to an inquiry from a Sacramento Bee staff writer regarding whether a music teacher who owns an instrument repair business violated conflict of interest laws by requesting his school district to contract with his company. The FPPC concludes that the music teacher violated Government Code §87100 by using his official position to influence a governmental decision.
